sentences.text: Additionally, the luck was there, because there was a-- the minute we escaped, we went through the wires.
The latrine.
The latrine.
And what happened actually-- there was an alarm-- an air alarm.
And the sirens stopped right away, and the lights stopped already.
Everything was out.
And we-- we did run quite a bit.
And when again the lights went up-- on, rather-- we were a little distance away.
And I knew exactly which way to go to the forest, because I used to go on the second floor where there was drying-out wood for the wheels.
It was elevated about the second store-- story.
And I used to look there.
And I knew I will go in the direction of a small railroad station by the name of Herby.
And over there the forest started.
Herby.
Herby.
H-E-R-B-Y.
And that's the way we did.
I was physically much stronger than Wasser, and Wasser-- I had to pull him and-- and help him out.
And finally, we reached the forest, still during the night.
And my feet were very bad.
We found a little stream.
I took off the shoes-- or the boots, rather-- and put my water-- my feet in the cold water-- felt a little better.

My plan was to go actually east, or to find the more [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] tolerant groups of partisans, which were socialists and supposedly had some Jewish members in their ranks, or to break through the front, through the forest, someplace.
That was my idea.
Besides that, I knew something like that.
I noticed the-- the Russian-- when they make an offensive, they paid attention to the dates-- historical dates.
There, I knew that June 22 they will make offensive.
The Russians started already coming towards Poland.
It was 1944, May '44.
The war started against Russia, by Hitler-- attack on the-- Saragossa-- you know, the-- the fight against Russia-- the war.
So the offensive started.
But they stopped about 50 miles from us.
And that was-- I miscalculated, or they didn't go any further.
Because they stopped before Warsaw, because they wanted to destroy the Polish underground.
You see, the Russian-- they stayed in Prague, across the Vistula River, and they didn't move.
And meanwhile, the German destroyed the-- the Poles made an insurrection at that time.
So I started to go, and we started east.
We going east, and through the villages and so forth.
We stopped at the Poles.
I spoke perfect Polish.
And we said that we escaped from the Germans.
They took us to work in Germany, and we going home.
I didn't let Wasser talk at all.
But one day we went-- he spoke with a little inflection-- the Yiddish inflection-- a little bit.
And we-- I asked for a direction-- where to go.
It was Sunday.
And the Poles were walking like that.
I said to them, we're looking for this-and-this village.
So Wasser started to say something.
So this woman, the lady, says to-- pointing on me-- you look like a Jew, but you speak perfect Polish.
He looks like a Pole, but he doesn't speak Polish!
[LAUGHS] Like that.
[LAUGHS] And so I said, he has a tongue tie.
And that's his problem with his diction like that.
We started to walk again.
And-- you know, and we walked, and we slept in-- in-- in barns.
We have a few-- not dollars-- we have a few zlotys.
We used to buy-- Poles didn't want to take money from us.
They used to give us some food and so forth.
Because I was-- I always talk.
And in one place, we came to a store.
We came to a store, and there was one man with the high boots and some kind-- semi uniform, but a civilian.
And he goes to Wasser, and he says, let me see your [INAUDIBLE] card.
Let me see your document, you know.
So Wasser had very good false papers.
He showed him.
He looked at it, and said OK.
And he said to me, where's you?
I took the pistol, and I shot in the ceiling.
And I said, that's my [INAUDIBLE] card.
That's my document.
Oh, she got-- he got startled, you see.
And then we got out of the store and started to move away.
And it was near the forest.
We went to the forest.
They started to run.
He already started to shoot, got some people there, and they started to run after us.
They started to run after us.
We went in the forest, and there was a-- a-- a clearing.
And there was two wagons-- an empty wagon and a peasant with a full wagon of potatoes.
I run to this Pole, and I said, take the horse, right here, put it to this empty wagon, and we go.
Through the road away, you see.
Wasser, from all the commotion, got diarrhea.
He had to go.
I said, right on the wagon.
He put his pants down and-- excuse me for the expression-- had to go there, and that's all.
I was standing by the man, by the Pole, with my gun.
I said, give me your documents.
OK?
I took it to me, and I said, if you ever say where we go out, your house and your barn will be out.
OK?
And when we saw them way, way back, far, we let them go, and we went in the forest again.
OK?
What forest was this?
[INAUDIBLE] Forest.
It was a-- a-- a big forest by my city.
The whole area was the [?
Swietok ?] Krzyz.
It was the Holy Cross Forest with a famous bald mountain over there.
There was a bald mountain there.
And even there was a big prison-- prison there-- very famous in Poland.
Swiety Krzyz was a very-- was a prison for-- for-- for severe criminals.
Yeah.
Of Polish?
S-W-I-E-T-Y K-R-Z-Y-Z.
But, you see, the 'Swiety'-- the [?
M ?] is-- is a soft.
You know, it's [NON-ENGLISH].
You pronounce [NON-ENGLISH].
And this K-R-Z is like a 'zhee'-- 'kshish.' You know.
And the Swietokrzyskie Mountains.
And after that, we saw that we have a tough situation.
I knew this partisan Kozubek-- K-O-Z-U-B-E-K-- who had-- had a contact with me.
And I said, you know-- to Wasser-- let's go to see Kozubek and see what he would tell us.
Maybe he will help us out to make contact with some Armia Ludowa.
That's folks army, you see, which was left-linked, you see.
And the peasants' platoons, also, they were more democratic, you see, and so forth.
So I knew where he lives-- know the village.
We went back, again-- went back, going west.
We went to the village.
And there was a-- a-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- smiths-- a smith-- open-- you know, horses, you know-- making the-- Horseshoes?
Horseshoes, yeah.
And we asked him, where's Kozubek's place?
Oh, he said, I am a cousin of his.
They're over there.
Go over there.
And we went to the village, and he was not there.
His wife was there.
And I said who I am.
I said, send somebody to--
He never slept in his house, because the-- the Gestapo was after him.
They knew that he's a partisan, you see?
So he never slept in the house.
After a little while, he came.
He came, and he said, come out with me.
He said, I help you out, but you have to go in the forest and meet a family who lives in the forest since 1942 there.
He took us in the forest, and there was a family-- a father, a mother, and a child of 16 years or 15 years old.
Joskowicz.
Jewish people, who his father told him, during the-- the [NON-ENGLISH], during the-- the action for Treblinka, he said, you don't go no place.
You go in the forest and stay there, because they kill you.
So those people were in the forest.
And he took us to them.
And so we said, OK, temporarily we'll stay here.
He said, stay here for a little while.
I make contact for you, and everything like that.
We came there.
You know, the guy never shaved.
Never-- he looked like a-- you know, really dirty and-- well, we helped him out.
We washed him.
There was a little bit-- a spring-- a white spring, we used to call it.
You see, there was a place where you used to come to it whenever there was some danger to us, you see, and we helped him out.
And we started to eat with them and be there.
And we thought we'll stay there for a day or two and then we'll go.
Well, he took a little longer.
Some other people started to come to us.
In Henrykow, in the place, there, you see, they knew-- the Jewish people, they knew that we are in the forest.
This Kozubek had contact there.
What happened-- this family had a nephew in Henrykow.
And this family helped out those in the forests.
And the Kozubek was the contact.
OK?
So they knew about us.
Someone from Ludwikow came out, you see.
He went [? visit ?] someplace so he didn't go back.
Finally, we wind up with 18 people in our group.
Beside Kozubek, we had another partisan of [?
Franek ?] [?
Czerwiec, ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who was a partisan.
I think he was a little bit more anti-Jewish partisan.
But he had rheumatic fever, and he was on leave from his unit, and he came to us, and he cooperated with us.

They used us, also, to help them-- to-- to attack some trains, destroy the narrow-gauge road-- railroad-- which carried those big trunks of wood to the city-- for-- for-- the-- to cut wood, you see, from the big forest-- from the-- from-- you know from the mountain.
And we used to also attack-- German took over big farms from the Poles.
And German took over.
And we used to attack for food.
Some cows, some geese, some stuff-- potatoes, you see-- and bring it in the forest, and that's all.
That's we used to live.
We never do anything to the local people, because we have to live with him, you see?
We didn't want to-- and we're-- they were poor like us, at that time, also.
And, we-- you know, this [?
Czerwiec ?] was a big, six-feet-something-tall guy.
He used to kill a German-- take his uniform.
He would put the uniform on his, you know.
And with the guns and everything.
We used to take the guns.
We put them in the forest, buried in a special box, a metal box, you see.
Whenever we needed the guns, we took them out.
We never-- only the pistols.
We used to have the short, you know, arms.
I was the leader.
I was the leader of the group, of [INAUDIBLE] Bronek.
I was.
Bronek.
They called me-- everybody knew me as Bronek, that's all.
Not Zelinger, not-- Bronek, that's all.
No.
No.
We-- I had a-- I had-- I lost it, but I had a tremendous sense of direction.
I could say that, if I start here and I go through this forest, I will deviate maybe-- maybe 500 yards or something like that.
Also, we used to go by the smell of the villages.
You know, depends on the wind, you see?
I knew exact, if the wind carried, which way to go at night.
We always walked on night-- at night.
We never walked-- in the forest, during the day, but we never go for attacking during the day-- nothing.
No, you see, we never make bunkers, because we had to move from place to place.
Because not only we had to deal with the Germans, with Ukrainians, with the Tatars-- they used the Tatars-- you know, these ethnic groups, you see?
The-- like Chechnya-- from Chechnya-- those people?
They used to attack us in the forest.
So we had to move from place to place.
So what we used to do for shelter, we had an idea like that.
Kielce was a big railroad hub.
And on-- on the railroad, there were big signs made out of tin-- big, big signs.
Let's say about, maybe, 4 meters by, you know, by 3 meters by 2 meters.
At night, we used to go tear off those-- those signs, take them in the forest, and we had roofs-- OK, many places, because we had to move from place to place.
Also what we used to do-- we took the-- we knew what-- we took the signs from here, put them over there, and those from over there, put them over here, so you confuse the people.
They didn't know where to go, the- the Germans.
That's what we did, you know-- for the joke of it.
But mainly, we used to have the roof made out of the signs of the rail-- what the Germans put up.
Because we had the Poles against us.
We have the-- the [?
Endecs-- ?] you know, those people who were rabid anti-Semites-- also against us.
They did actually cooperate with the Germans.
There were some groups in Poland of the-- they used to call them [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]-- the national-- national armed powers.
They already cooperated in Germans-- with Germans against the Jews and maybe against the-- the left-linked Poles, too.
They were Poles, actually, you see?
So we had to-- really was squeezed.
One of the guys who came to my group was a manufacturer of ladies' shoes.
During the war, beginning of the war, he had 200 pairs of shoes put in a false soffit or whatever in his apartment or whatever.
He told this to Kozubek-- to one of my people, of the partisans.
And this Kozubek was greedy.
He took a wagon-- a horse-drawn wagon-- and he went there to get those shoes.
It so happened that the biggest murderer, [?
Rumpel, ?] lived-- had a girlfriend in this building.
And he got caught there, while doing it.
They took him on the Jewish cemetery, and they killed him there, and he's married-- buried on the Jewish cemetery, this Kozubek.
Czerwiec. ?] I lost [?
Czerwiec, ?] too, because [?
Czerwiec ?] was supposed to get the order that he is supposed to go to Romania, from Romania to Italy.
Whatever happened to him, I don't know.
So we are left without those two people, by the end.
And it was bad.
So I decided, again, to try to force through to the more positive groups which could help us out.
I made contact with somebody who said-- he said, somebody there in Daleszyce, in a-- in a village, who has contact with the peasants' army.
OK.
We went there-- knocked on the door.
A woman opened, and the man was there.
And I told them what it is.
Oh, yes, I know.
And he said, what do you want?
I said, we want to make contact with the Armia Ludowa.
Oh, he said, I know what it is, you see.
Let me take you to the forest, and we'll make-- I call another man with me.
We'll go to-- to the bunker.
So they went like that, without anything.
They went there.
And they put it in a bunker.
There was a little hill, and there was a deep bunker.
And they say, you wait here, and we'll come to get-- make contact with you.
So, when they left, those two guys left, I said to Wasser, who was with me, I said, you know, I'm suspicious.
I don't know what they know.
I said, you know what?
Let's go back, behind the trees, and see what happens.
So we stand behind big trees there, and I saw three people coming with the guns, like that.
They go with the guns like this.
Rifles.
And one guy-- two stand by the entrance of the bunker, and one guy goes in inside.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I knew that something-- they want to do something.
So I looked a little bit more, and probably they noticed me-- one.
And he shot me right here.
Right by the heart.
I had some documents here.
Maybe this deflected a little bit the bullet-- and broke my rib.
OK?
I started to cough.
But I have to come back-- tell you about the grenade.
I had a grenade in my hand.
Where did you get it?
And I threw the grenade.
And everything got quiet.
If I killed them, if I didn't kill him, I don't know.
We started to run, and we survived.
OK.
The grenade I got that way.
We are sitting in the forest, one afternoon-- or it was late in the afternoon.
And somebody surrounds us.
One of the men says, grenade.
Grenade.
They threw a grenade.
And they-- one guy comes to me right away with a submachine gun.
OK?
He had a tattoo here of a Polish bird-- you know, the national bird-- the eagle.
OK?
He had a WP-- means Walczaca Polska-- the Fighting Poland, you see?
And he knew about me.
OK?
He said, where's your gun?
He saw my gun here.
He took the gun.
Where are the bullets?
I got 35 bullets, already.
They threw the bullets from a plane, and we found one of those magazines with nine-millimeters bullets.
He took away from me.
He took me.
The rest dispersed.
He took me.
He took another three-- three guys.
Starczynski, ?] [?
Bojgen-- ?] four-- four guys-- [?
Starczynski, ?] [?
Bojgen-- ?] oh, I forget the name.
Anyway, he took four guys, and they arrested us.
They didn't tie us up-- nothing.
There were him and another three soldiers with rifles.
Polish.
And we're going like that.
And I knew the forest like-- nobody could say what it is, you see-- tell me where to go, or something like that.
And we go, and I talk to him-- what do you want to do with us?
Why did you arrest me?
Why'd you take my thing?
I didn't do anything to you.
I am here.
I am doing the job against the Germans, the same like I do.
If you want me to work with you, I will.
He said, you know, we have a Jewish boy who works in the kitchen.
OK.
So he said, fine.
Meanwhile, when we go, one guy comes, and he says, here, or [? wide-- ?] or further?
So I knew something is not kosher.
Because he think maybe to kill us here or to go further.
And there was the narrow railroad track.
And from time to time, the Germans used to go on a special wagon-- police-- to check if everything OK, since sabotage and so forth.
And then make a signal.
Before they came, they made a signal.
So I-- so-- so they-- when they heard the signal from the distance, I said, we should lie down.
And he kneeled down and kept the submachine gun by my head.
OK?
I lift up my head.
He said, put your snout to the ground-- in the ground.
OK, so like that.
And I knew something like that.
I said to myself, look, if they will-- German will come-- it's a little clearing-- if I will try to escape, they will not shoot, because they will not be able to fight with them.
Meanwhile, when we walked, I talked to the boys in Jewish-- in Yiddish-- I said, [YIDDISH].
I mean, do it on your own.
I cannot help you anymore.
OK?
Whatever you can do.
You know.
And I decided like that.
If I escape, try to escape, and they shoot me in the back, at least they kill me.
OK?
I don't want to stand and be killed like that.
And the minute when I saw this coming like that, he moved a little bit to look like that.
I tore up and turned around and started to run, zig-zag.
If they shoot after me or not, I don't know.
I'm here.
I survived.
The rest of the boys were killed.
In fact, the [INAUDIBLE] found out that one of the boys, [?
Strawczynski, ?] had a $20 gold coin in his underwear.
They found it.
They took it.
I have some court proceedings-- they talk about it, about those people killed by the forest, there.
And, you know, that's-- OK.
So when I came-- when I came, they saw me.
They thought that I came from-- from someplace, that that was not me.
The rest of the people.
They thought that some kind of a miracle happened, that I survived.
Well, I came back, and I saw this grenade there.
What happened-- the grenade handle didn't open.
So when they threw the grenade, it got stucked in the mud, this way.
So I said, move away.
Everybody moves away.
I went there and, careful, caught the handle, closed it like this, put a piece of wire before I made it, and made the pin.
And I had the grenade.
OK?
And this grenade saved us our life, me and Wasser's, when we tried to make contact again.
But now you were shot.
What happened to you?
Oh, this-- I coughed.
I thought, you know, if I have to die now-- nothing happened to me.
The bullet went from here down-- down.
And it was took out-- they took it out after the war, when the Russian came.
I had the bullet, and I gave it to my wife.
She carried it on a-- on a chain.
And whatever happened to it, I don't know.
It was a copper bullet-- a Polish bullet, not a German.
A Polish bullet.
Steel.
Steel.
The-- the-- the cover was steel, you see.
Grey, or steel.
[INAUDIBLE].
So we came back there, and we saw we were surrounded like that without any, you know-- and everything already was dispersed.
The-- the-- the people started to come in the forest, you see, because the Russians started to attack.
We saw already the-- the artillery fire from the distant, that night, you see.
And it was very cold.
And when we came back-- I'm-- I'm going back, now, the second time, because I regressed a little with the grenade.
This happened in-- in August-- in August of '44.
OK?
I'm talking now about November '44.
OK?
When we came back there-- because it took us a long time to try to penetrate, and so forth-- I came back, and I saw those two guys, OK, dying.
They had a frostbite and gangrene.
Two members of my group.
OK?
And they were dying.
Nothing-- there was no medical care, and so forth.
And they did die a few days later.
And we-- the ground was frozen already.
We buried them in a shallow grave.
You know, and I think this Paul, who wrote me this 26-page letter-- he described, you know, about the bones or whatever-- their graves that you see, that's all.
And there was already disorganization.
The Polish parties were already dispersed, after Warsaw insurrection.
The Russian were not far, and we already said, OK, we won't do anything anymore.
We try to survive.
And by January, we started to see, you know, artillery and-- and-- and the planes coming over.
And we're sitting underneath the tree, and the upper part of the tree was shot through the-- the-- with an artillery projectile-- fell down, you know-- the whole-- half of the tree.
Nothing happened to us.
And we heard some Germans going that way.
There was an old road, which used to be a road to Warsaw from my city, all through the forest.
And it was German.
We could hear, from the distant, German yelling and so forth.
Then, from this side, I started to note, I hear something different.
I said, I'm going to see.
You want to go see with--?
They were afraid to go.
It was pitch-- it was night and so forth.
I said, I will go myself.
I go out, and I see a scene like that.
There are tanks, and there are dog-pulled sled with wounded people.
And I go there, and I see Russian tanks.
And I go to one guy.
I said, [RUSSIAN]-- Russian.
He looked at me-- said, where are you from?
I said, I'm partisan.
Oh, he said, [RUSSIAN]!
That means 'Good for you,' you see.
And they let me go.
Then I went to them back, you see, and I told them this situation.
They didn't believe me.
They didn't believe me.
They said they wait until day and then go out.
So I said, fine.
If you want that way, stay here, and I go to Kozubek's house-- to his wife, you know.
And I went there to Kozubek's wife.
And they-- the kids used to play with some-- found some guns and so forth.
I said, don't touch it, because it will explode, and so forth.
But they used to play.
And then the guys came.
I stayed there-- wait for them.
And we started to go to the city-- to the town.
And when we went to the town, we used to go in the middle of the streets, because there was a lot of rubbish and destruction and so forth.
And we could see Poles pointing to us.
Oh, look at those-- those Jews!
Hitler didn't finish him.
Look at this!
Where did they come from?
You know?
And I felt some kind of a hostility.
And-- and-- and they were surprised, you see.
And I came to the city.
It was day.
It was cold.
And I said, what should we do here?
So we ask a soldier, where's the headquarters?
They said, over there.
So there was a-- a-- a big building where the Polish IRS had a-- a annex or a building there.
We went there, and there was the headquarters of the Polish army attached to the Russians.
And we went there.
We said, we're hungry.
We want to eat.
We came from the forest.
So they gave us something to eat.
We can use you.
So they took us on the motorcycles to their headquarters.
And the headquarters was the area's headquarters of the-- of the security-- Polish security.
And they took us there.
We started to translate the dossiers of-- of spies and ethnic Germans who did cooperate and so forth, you see?
And that was-- I got to be a rank of a lieutenant, with a uniform.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I didn't like it.
